items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.q: How many semitones are in a perfect fifth?
items.why: Students count fifth as 'five steps' but the name refers to letter NAMES (1-2-3-4-5), not semitones. C to G spans 5 letter names but 7 semitones (C-C#-D-D#-E-F-F#-G).
Students confuse andante (walking, moderate) with adagio (slow). Andante literally means 'going/walking' in Italian - it's a comfortable pace, not slow.
The answer examiners want is: Schoenberg. “Stravinsky” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with minimalist works such as 'Music for 18 Musicians'?
items.why: It's Reich. “Glass” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did John Cage compose his silent work '4'33"'?
items.why: The real figure is 1952. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
